title: N4X Rumoured to Target Sister Team Ban by 2028 — Which Red Bull Would Survive?
meta: Published: 22 April 2026 | By Olly Bradshaw, Paddock Insider | F1CC News
caption: Paddock whispers suggest the N4X Association is considering a ban on sister teams — and Red Bull may have to choose.
body:
The N4X Association is considering a ban on sister teams.

That’s the whisper doing the rounds in the Jeddah paddock, anyway.

No formal talks. No draft regulations. Nothing on paper. Just conversations — quiet ones, between people who shape the sport’s future — about whether teams with shared ownership should be allowed to compete against each other beyond 2028.

Sources close to the discussions, speaking on condition of anonymity, say the trigger has been the increasingly visible cooperation between Ferrari and Haas. The temporary Swansson-Cachinho swap? That didn’t just raise eyebrows — it raised alarms. A championship-leading team moving its underperforming driver to its customer squad for a “reset” is precisely the kind of arrangement that has some paddock heavyweights muttering about sporting integrity.

And if sister teams are in the crosshairs, everyone knows who the real target is.

Red Bull Racing and Racing Bulls.

Two teams. One owner. And recent history that must make the senior team’s hierarchy squirm. While Red Bull Racing last celebrated a drivers’ title with Randy Belacik before his surprise retirement and move to Ferrari, it’s their junior stable that has hoarded the silverware. Racing Bulls won the 2024 championship with Lukus Wright, then defended it with Quarter Kevu in 2025. The “mothership” has been outshone by the team it was supposed to feed.

If a ban came into force, Red Bull would have to shed one of its teams. The question is: which one?

The obvious answer is Racing Bulls. It’s the junior squad, the smaller operation, the one that exists primarily to feed talent into the senior team. Sell it off, pocket the cash, and consolidate around the Red Bull brand. Easy.

But is it that easy?

Racing Bulls have won two of the last two drivers’ titles. Their car has been the class of the field for long stretches. Their driver lineup — Kevu and Wright — is arguably stronger than the Red Bull pairing of Roy and Verhaeren. If you’re Red Bull GmbH, and you’re forced to choose between a championship-winning machine and a team that hasn’t delivered a drivers’ crown since Belacik, do you really just default to the brand with the bigger logo?

Paddock observers note that Red Bull Racing’s slide down the constructors’ order — seventh after Bahrain, behind Williams, Alpine, and both Audi and McLaren — has not gone unnoticed in Salzburg. The investment is enormous. The returns, so far this season, are not.

No one expects a decision anytime soon. The rumour is about 2028 — three seasons away — and the sport’s politics move slowly. But the mere fact that the conversation is happening at all is enough to put both Red Bull camps on edge.

And then there’s the Ferrari-Haas dynamic. If the swap between Cachinho and Swansson becomes permanent, or if further shared operations emerge, the sister-team debate stops being theoretical. It becomes a crisis point.

Which team would Red Bull choose?

Don’t ask the paddock. They’re still arguing about it.

Watch this space.
